episode_title: When The Diagnosis Is Thrown For A Lupus
host: Dr. Jessica Steier & Dr. Sarah Scheinman
summary: Dr. Roberto Caricchio discusses systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), a complex autoimmune disease affecting predominantly women that can involve multiple organ systems. The episode covers lupus pathogenesis, diagnostic challenges, genetic and environmental risk factors (including hormones, UV exposure, viral infections, and microbiome changes), and recent breakthrough treatments including CAR-T cell therapy that can induce long-term clinical remission. Dr. Caricchio emphasizes the unprecedented expansion of treatment options and clinical trials now available for lupus patients, representing a major shift from 50 years of therapeutic stagnation.
science_topics: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) pathogenesis and multi-organ involvement
Autoimmune disease mechanisms and autoantibody production (anti-SM, anti-double-stranded DNA antibodies)
Genetic predisposition in lupus (single nucleotide polymorphisms, monogenic lupus)
Sex hormone influences on lupus disease severity (estrogen and androgen effects on B cell activation)
Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection as lupus trigger
Lupus microbiome composition and pro-inflammatory characteristics
UV radiation as lupus disease trigger and flare inducer
CAR-T cell therapy and cellular immunotherapy for lupus
Immune system reset mechanisms and immune reconstitution
Discoid lupus erythematosus (DLE) and cutaneous lupus subtypes
Concordance studies in identical twins for genetic component estimation
claims_to_note: Lupus diagnosis currently takes months to years; previously took 2-3 years to diagnose (improved from past decade)
30% concordance rate in identical twins indicates strong but incomplete genetic component
Approximately 9 out of 10 lupus patients are women (90% female predominance)
More than 90% of world population infected with EBV, but EBV increases lupus development risk
No FDA-approved lupus treatments existed for 50 years until first approval ~20 years ago
Three additional FDA-approved lupus interventions approved in recent years, with possibly two more in current year (2026)
CAR-T cell therapy first lupus patient treated in Germany in 2021, still in remission without treatment as of 2026
First CAR-T cell therapy lupus patient in New England treated in 2023, still lupus-free and treatment-free after ~3 years
Approximately 150 ongoing clinical trials for lupus and lupus nephritis worldwide
40+ clinical trials currently underway testing cell therapy modalities for lupus
CAR-T therapy achieves 'clinical cure' (disease-free, drug-free remission) but not complete cure due to persistent genetic predisposition
policy_relevance: FDA approval process and regulatory pathway for lupus treatments
NIH funding for lupus research (Dr. Caricchio's lab is NIH-funded)
Lupus Clinical Investigators Network (LCIN) - clinical research program overseen by Lupus Therapeutics
Lupus Research Alliance sponsoring research and supporting clinical trial pathway development
ClinicalTrials.gov as government-run resource for identifying lupus clinical trials
Multi-center institutional collaboration pathway for establishing CAR-T cell therapy programs (published guidance for investigators)
Need for healthcare infrastructure to expand access to complex cell therapy treatments beyond specialized centers
key_quotes: Lupus is the quintessential systemic autoimmune disease... the same immune system that helps us defending ourselves from infections, mistakenly thinks that actually our own body is one of those entities and it attacks them.

What has happened and what's happening in lupus is truly unprecedented... we had nothing for 50 years. What was approved on those 50 years actually was really grandfathered into FDA because they were used before even the FDA was actually created.

This type of treatment really get rid of those autoimmune cells in the tissue where the disease is... And then those cells will be reproduced by the bone marrow. But when they come back, they are no longer autoimmune. They are no longer induce the disease. It did give us a pathway to a cure.

publications.title: Oil in the Sea IV: Inputs, Fates, and Effects
publications.description: Oil and natural gas represent more than 50 percent of the worldwide energy supply, with high energy demand driven by population growth and improving standards of living. Despite significant progress in reducing the amount of oil in the sea from consumption, exploration, transportation, and production, risks remain. This report, the fourth in a series, documents the current state-of-knowledge on inputs, fates and effects of oil in the sea, reflecting almost 20 additional years of research, including long-term effects from spills such as the Exxon Valdez and a decade-long boom in oil spill science research following the Deepwater Horizon oil spill. The report finds that land-based sources of oil are the biggest input of oil to the sea, far outweighing other sources, and it also notes that the effects of chronic inputs on the marine environment, such as land-based runoff, are very different than that from an acute input, such as a spill. publications.title: What You Need to Know About Infectious Disease
The Influence of Global Environmental Change on Infectious Disease Dynamics: Workshop Summary
publications.description: The twentieth century witnessed an era of unprecedented, large-scale, anthropogenic changes to the natural environment. Understanding how environmental factors directly and indirectly affect the emergence and spread of infectious disease has assumed global importance for life on this planet. While the causal links between environmental change and disease emergence are complex, progress in understanding these links, as well as how their impacts may vary across space and time, will require transdisciplinary, transnational, collaborative research. This research may draw upon the expertise, tools, and approaches from a variety of disciplines. Such research may inform improvements in global readiness and capacity for surveillance, detection, and response to emerging microbial threats to plant, animal, and human health. Genomic Epidemiology Data Infrastructure Needs for SARS-CoV-2: Modernizing Pandemic Response Strategies
publications.description: In December 2019, new cases of severe pneumonia were first detected in Wuhan, China, and the cause was determined to be a novel beta coronavirus related to the severe acute respiratory syndrome (SARS) coronavirus that emerged from a bat reservoir in 2002. Within six months, this new virusâ€"SARS coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2)â€"has spread worldwide, infecting at least 10 million people with an estimated 500,000 deaths. COVID-19, the disease caused by SARS-CoV-2, was declared a public health emergency of international concern on January 30, 2020 by the World Health Organization (WHO) and a pandemic on March 11, 2020. To date, there is no approved effective treatment or vaccine for COVID-19, and it continues to spread in many countries.
